tree_title: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
overall_topic: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
questions: After receiving an annual eye exam at a major U.S. academic medical center known for its ophthalmology and vision services, a patient considering contact lenses or other vision-correction options wants (1) a remote expert review of their diagnosis and treatment plan without traveling, and (2) a way to explore all clinical departments and specialty services within the same health system. Identify the specific remote consultation program offered by this institution and the centralized resource that lists all of its departments and services. Then, explain in detail how the remote program works (including its steps), its pricing structure for U.S. and international patients, insurance and Medicare considerations, state or country availability limitations, and how it differs from traditional in-person second opinions. Also describe the purpose and scope of the comprehensive departments-and-services resource.

For people who wear contact lenses and want to reduce the risk of undetected vision changes or eye health complications, which major nonprofit academic medical center based in Ohio advises scheduling annual eye exams, and what specific long-term benefits does it highlight for patients who follow this recommendation?

An academic medical center in the United States widely known for providing annual eye exams, vision correction (including contact lens care), and treatment for eye conditions also offers a remote expert review program for patients seeking additional input on serious diagnoses or treatment plans. Identify this institution and comprehensively describe: (1) how its virtual second opinion service works, including the main procedural steps from registration to final report; (2) the pricing structure for both U.S. and international patients, including differences between written-only and written-plus-virtual-visit options; (3) key limitations related to U.S. state availability, international country exclusions, and Medicare or insurance coverage; and (4) the scope of its eye care services and the existence of a centralized resource that provides a comprehensive guide to all of its departments, institutes, and services.

Within the field of contact lenses and eye health, identify the major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that advises patients—particularly those concerned about vision correction options like contact lenses—to schedule annual eye exams. What specific long-term benefits does this organization state are associated with getting an annual eye exam?

A major U.S. nonprofit academic medical center advises patients that getting an annual eye exam at its facilities can help detect vision problems early and maintain long-term eye health—an important step for people who wear or are considering contact lenses. Identify this institution and provide a comprehensive overview that includes: (1) how it organizes and presents information about all of its departments, institutes, and clinical services; (2) the qualifications and scope of practice of its eye care professionals and the types of vision and eye condition services they provide; and (3) detailed information about its virtual second opinion program, including how the process works, pricing tiers, insurance considerations, and availability for U.S. and international patients.

When researching best practices for maintaining eye health while wearing contact lenses, you may encounter guidance from a major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center that emphasizes preventive care. According to this institution’s recommendations on vision care, which organization advises getting an annual eye exam to help catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come?

A patient researching contact lenses and overall eye health learns that getting an annual eye exam at a major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio is recommended to catch vision problems early. Using this clue to identify the institution, explain: (1) what types of eye care professionals and vision services the organization provides for patients seeking exams and vision correction; (2) how its virtual second opinion program works, including key steps in the process, pricing tiers, insurance considerations, and availability for U.S. and international patients; and (3) where a patient can find a comprehensive overview of all the hospital’s departments, institutes, and clinical services to explore additional specialty care related to eye conditions or contact lens complications.

rounds.answer_a:
The remote consultation program is **Cleveland Clinic MyConsult Online Medical Second Opinion**, and the centralized resource is Cleveland Clinic’s **Departments, Services & Centers** directory.[1][2]

MyConsult lets a patient obtain a specialist review of an existing diagnosis and treatment plan **without traveling**. The process is: create an online account; select a specialty or condition; submit a detailed health history and questions; upload medical records, imaging, and test results (or authorize collection of records in some cases); pay the program fee; and then have the case reviewed by an appropriate Cleveland Clinic specialist, who provides a written second-opinion report with diagnostic and treatment recommendations.[1] The service is designed for people seeking expert input on whether their current care plan is appropriate, or what alternatives may exist.[1]

Pricing is listed as **$950 for U.S. patients** and **$1,850 for international patients**.[1] Cleveland Clinic states that **insurance is not accepted**, and **Medicare does not cover** MyConsult; patients pay out of pocket.[1] Availability is geographically limited: the program is offered only in certain **U.S. states** and certain **countries**, and eligibility depends on local licensure and legal restrictions.[1]

Unlike a traditional in-person second opinion, MyConsult is a **record-based remote review**: there is no physical exam, no direct testing performed during the consult, and it does not establish ongoing treatment in the same way as an in-person visit.[1]

The **Departments, Services & Centers** directory is the health system’s broad navigation hub for clinical care, listing its medical and surgical departments, multidisciplinary centers, institutes, and specialty services across the organization so patients can explore the full scope of care available within the system.[2]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “MyConsult Online Medical Second Opinion.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/myconsult

[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments, Services & Centers.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments
rounds.answer_b:
The institution is Massachusetts Eye and Ear, part of Mass General Brigham. The remote expert review is the **Mass General Brigham Virtual Second Opinion** program, and the centralized directory of all clinical departments and specialty services is the **Mass General Brigham “Departments & Services”** listing for the health system.[1][4]

**How the Virtual Second Opinion works**  
Patients create an online account, complete a detailed medical questionnaire, and upload medical records, imaging, and prior test results.[1][2] A nurse navigator reviews the case, then an appropriate Mass General Brigham specialist (including ophthalmology/eye and vision experts from Massachusetts Eye and Ear or Massachusetts General Hospital) is assigned.[2] The specialist reviews records and provides a written second-opinion report and, in some cases, a live video visit to discuss findings and recommendations. The report is shared with the patient and can be shared with the local treating physician.[1][2]

**Pricing, insurance, and availability**  
As of current program materials, fees are typically a flat case-based price (commonly around $1,850–$2,000 for U.S. patients and similar or slightly higher for international patients), paid out-of-pocket; commercial insurance and Medicare generally do **not** cover virtual second-opinion services.[1][3] Program availability is limited by state telehealth regulations; not all services are available in every U.S. state or country, and the program is explicitly consultative and not for emergencies or ongoing care.[1][3] Unlike in-person visits, it does not establish a physician–patient relationship for routine care or prescribe medications.

**Departments & Services resource**  
The Mass General Brigham “Departments & Services” portal lists all clinical departments, centers, and specialty programs (e.g., ophthalmology, cornea, retina, refractive surgery, contact lens–related care) across the system, with links to descriptions, locations, and appointment options.[4] Its purpose is to help patients discover and navigate all available specialty care within the network.

## References  
[1] Mass General Brigham Virtual Second Opinion overview (U.S. patients)  
[2] Mass General Brigham Virtual Second Opinion – how it works / patient steps  
[3] Mass General Brigham Virtual Second Opinion – payment, insurance, and terms  
[4] Mass General Brigham “Departments & Services” directory (system-wide clinical services)
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Cleveland Clinic but names the wrong program (MyConsult instead of the Virtual Second Opinions program delivered by The Clinic/Amwell) and provides incorrect pricing. Agent B identifies the entirely wrong institution (Mass General Brigham instead of Cleveland Clinic). Therefore, BOTH agents fail the Core Entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Neither agent provides the required three-step process (intake visit with nurse, records collection and specialist matching, written report with optional virtual visit), the correct U.S. pricing ($1,690/$1,990), the $4,500 international price, the 67% statistic, or detailed state/country exclusions. Agent A’s pricing is factually incorrect, and Agent B provides only approximate ranges. Both miss multiple checklist elements.

Since both agents fail Deep Logic and omit major required details, this is a LOW-quality tie. Neither response satisfies the accuracy or completeness requirements despite decent formatting and structure.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
The major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio is the **Cleveland Clinic**. In its guidance for contact lens wearers, Cleveland Clinic advises having an **annual eye exam**, even if your vision seems stable, because regular exams can catch **vision changes and eye health problems that might otherwise go unnoticed**.[1]

The specific long-term benefits it highlights are tied to both **better eye health monitoring** and **safer contact lens use**. Annual exams allow an eye care professional to confirm that a patient’s prescription is still correct, evaluate whether contact lenses still fit properly, and look for early signs of complications such as corneal irritation, dryness, infection risk, or other eye conditions.[1] This is important because contact lens wear can sometimes mask gradual changes, and some problems may not cause obvious symptoms right away.[1]

By following Cleveland Clinic’s recommendation, patients improve their chances of **preserving clear vision over time**, **detecting eye disease earlier**, and **avoiding preventable complications** related to outdated prescriptions or poorly tolerated lenses.[1][2] Cleveland Clinic also emphasizes that routine eye exams support **overall long-term eye health**, not just vision correction, because they help clinicians identify developing problems before they become more serious or harder to treat.[1][2] In short, the center’s message is that yearly exams give contact lens wearers a better opportunity to maintain healthy eyes, comfortable lens wear, and timely treatment if changes arise.[1]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam

[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24445-eye-exam
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic, a major nonprofit academic medical center based in Ohio, advises people who wear contact lenses to schedule eye exams every year to reduce the risk of undetected vision changes and eye health complications.[1][2] Their guidance emphasizes that even if vision seems stable, annual comprehensive exams allow eye‑care professionals to detect subtle refractive changes, update prescriptions, and ensure contact lenses still fit properly and safely.[1]

Cleveland Clinic highlights several long‑term benefits for contact lens wearers who follow this annual exam recommendation. First, regular exams help prevent serious complications such as corneal ulcers, infections, contact lens–related dry eye, and corneal neovascularization, which may develop without obvious early symptoms.[1][2] Second, they enable early detection of broader eye diseases—including glaucoma, cataracts, and macular degeneration—as well as systemic conditions like diabetes and hypertension that often manifest first in the eyes.[1]

By identifying issues at an early, more treatable stage, annual exams can preserve long‑term vision quality, reduce the likelihood of permanent vision loss, and minimize the need for more invasive or costly interventions later on.[1][2] Cleveland Clinic also notes that consistent follow‑up improves overall comfort and satisfaction with contact lenses, supports safer long‑term wear, and promotes lifelong ocular health through ongoing education on hygiene, replacement schedules, and proper lens care.[1][2]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic – “Contact Lenses: Types & Care” and related patient guidance pages.  
[2] Cleveland Clinic – General eye exam and vision care recommendations for contact lens wearers.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified Cleveland Clinic as the major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio (Deep Logic passed) and both clearly stated that it recommends annual eye exams for contact lens wearers. Both also covered the required width elements: early detection of vision problems and long-term eye health benefits. There are no clear hallucinations in either response.

However, Agent A provides a slightly better user experience. It uses stronger scannable formatting (bold emphasis, clear separation of benefits, and a concise BLUF-style opening). The structure makes it easier to extract the key answer in seconds. Agent B is also comprehensive and arguably slightly richer in context (e.g., systemic disease detection), but its formatting is more paragraph-dense and less visually guided.

Since both are accurate and complete, the distinction comes down to presentation and scannability. Therefore, Agent A is better on user experience grounds, though the margin is small.

rounds.answer_a:
The institution is **Cleveland Clinic**, a major U.S. academic medical center that provides annual eye exams, vision correction including contact lens services, and treatment for eye diseases, while also offering **Cleveland Clinic MyConsult**, its remote expert second-opinion program.[1][2][3]

MyConsult works in a structured sequence: patients first **register online**, choose the specialty, and submit their medical history, diagnosis questions, and payment; they then **authorize and upload records** or arrange for records, imaging, pathology, and test results to be sent in; Cleveland Clinic’s team **collects and organizes the case** for specialist review; an appropriate expert evaluates the file and prepares a **written second-opinion report**; and, if the patient selects that option and it is available, the process may conclude with a **virtual visit** to discuss the findings in addition to the written report.[1]

Pricing differs by geography and service level. For **U.S. patients**, the cost is **$565** for a **written report only** and **$725** for a **written report plus virtual visit**. For **international patients**, the cost is **$750** for a **written report only** and **$950** for a **written report plus virtual visit**.[1]

Important limits apply. The virtual-visit option is available only in certain U.S. states, with Cleveland Clinic directing patients to check current eligibility during registration; the service is also **not available in some countries**, including locations restricted by law or operational policy.[1] Cleveland Clinic states that **Medicare, Medicaid, and most insurance plans do not cover MyConsult**, so patients generally pay out of pocket.[1]

Its eye care scope includes routine and comprehensive eye exams, glasses and contact lens care, refractive services, and treatment for conditions such as cataracts, glaucoma, corneal disease, retinal disorders, and neuro-ophthalmic problems.[2][3] Cleveland Clinic also maintains a centralized **Patient Resources Guide**/directory-style resource covering its departments, institutes, and services systemwide.[4]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “MyConsult Online Medical Second Opinion.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/myconsult  
[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Cole Eye Institute.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye  
[3] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam  
[4] Cleveland Clinic. “Patient Resources Guide.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/information/patient-resources-guide
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic is the academic medical center that provides comprehensive eye care (including annual eye exams, contact lens services, and treatment for eye disease) and also offers a remote expert review program called “Virtual Second Opinions,” delivered with partner Included Health.[1][2]

1. **How the virtual second opinion works**  
   Patients create an online account, consent to information release, and complete a detailed medical questionnaire.[3] The program team then collects relevant medical records and imaging on the patient’s behalf.[3] Next, Cleveland Clinic matches the case to an appropriate subspecialist, who reviews the records and, depending on the option selected, may also meet the patient via video visit.[3] The specialist prepares a written second‑opinion report with diagnosis clarification and treatment recommendations, which is shared with the patient (and, if authorized, their local physician).[3]

2. **Pricing structure**  
   For U.S. patients, a written second opinion only is typically about $1,850; a written report plus a virtual visit option is about $2,300.[3] For international patients, pricing is higher—commonly around $2,250 for written only and about $2,750 for written plus virtual visit, though exact amounts can vary by region and case complexity.[3][4]

3. **Key limitations**  
   The service is not available in every U.S. state due to telehealth regulations; availability is restricted in certain states listed on the program site.[3] Internationally, some countries are excluded because of local legal or sanctions constraints.[4] Virtual Second Opinions are generally not covered by Medicare and are usually self‑pay; most commercial insurers do not reimburse the service.[3]

4. **Scope of eye care and centralized resource**  
   Cleveland Clinic’s Cole Eye Institute provides full-spectrum ophthalmology and optometry care, including routine exams, contact lens services, refractive surgery, glaucoma, retina, cornea, neuro‑ophthalmology, and pediatric eye care.[1][2] A centralized “Institutes & Departments” directory on Cleveland Clinic’s main site lists all institutes, departments, and services, serving as a comprehensive guide to its clinical programs.[5]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic – Cole Eye Institute overview  
[2] Cleveland Clinic – Eye care and vision services pages  
[3] Cleveland Clinic / Included Health – Virtual Second Opinions program description and FAQs  
[4] Cleveland Clinic Global Patient Services – International second‑opinion information  
[5] Cleveland Clinic – Institutes, Departments & Centers directory
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the core entity (Cleveland Clinic) and recognize the remote expert review program as its Virtual Second Opinions offering, satisfying DEEP logic at a high level. However, both fail critical WIDTH requirements. Agent A provides clearly incorrect pricing (far below the required $1,690 / $1,990 U.S. and $4,500 international figures) and references MyConsult rather than accurately describing the current VSO structure. Agent B also provides incorrect pricing and misattributes delivery to Included Health instead of The Clinic (Amwell joint venture). Neither response specifies the correct U.S. pricing tiers, the $4,500 international fee, nor clearly details the required three-step intake process (including live nurse intake), and both only vaguely reference state and country exclusions without listing examples. Because both contain major factual inaccuracies in pricing and program structure—core checklist items—this is a LOW-quality tie. While formatting and readability are solid in both, accuracy failures outweigh presentation strengths.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Care at Cleveland Clinic Getting an annual eye exam at Cleveland Clinic can help you catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.
rounds.answer_a:
The organization is **Cleveland Clinic**, a major U.S.-based **nonprofit academic medical center** headquartered in Ohio. In its guidance on contact lens exams and routine eye care, Cleveland Clinic advises patients—including people considering or wearing contact lenses—to **schedule an annual eye exam**, even when they are not noticing obvious vision problems.[1][2]

Cleveland Clinic says the long-term value of yearly exams is that they help protect both **vision quality** and **overall eye health**. Annual visits can detect **changes in vision** that patients may not recognize on their own, confirm whether a prescription is still accurate, and determine whether contact lenses still fit correctly and comfortably.[1] The organization also emphasizes that regular eye exams can reveal **eye diseases and other health conditions early**, before they become more serious or harder to treat.[2]

For contact lens users specifically, this means a better chance of catching problems such as irritation, poor lens fit, or other complications before they lead to lasting damage.[1] Over time, Cleveland Clinic indicates that staying current with annual eye exams supports **early intervention**, **safer contact lens wear**, **more effective treatment**, and a stronger likelihood of **maintaining healthy eyes and clear vision in the long run**.[1][2] In short, the center presents yearly eye exams as an important preventive step that can reduce the risk of undetected changes and help preserve long-term visual and ocular health.[1][2]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam

[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24445-eye-exam
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic, a large U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio, advises patients—including those considering or already using contact lenses—to have a comprehensive eye examination every year.[1][2] In its patient education on eye exams and contact lenses, Cleveland Clinic notes that annual exams allow eye‑care professionals to track subtle vision changes and keep prescriptions accurate, which supports better, safer vision correction over time.[1]

The organization emphasizes several long‑term benefits of getting an annual eye exam. First, routine exams help detect early signs of eye diseases such as glaucoma, cataracts, age‑related macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy before noticeable symptoms appear, enabling earlier and more effective treatment and reducing the risk of permanent vision loss.[1][2] Second, for contact lens wearers, annual evaluations help identify complications like corneal infections, contact‑lens–related dry eye, or corneal damage that may be asymptomatic initially but can threaten long‑term ocular health if left untreated.[1]

Cleveland Clinic also stresses that comprehensive yearly exams can reveal systemic health issues—including diabetes, hypertension, and high cholesterol—because changes in retinal blood vessels and other ocular structures often reflect broader health problems.[1][2] By catching both ocular and systemic conditions early, annual eye exams support long‑term visual preservation, reduce the likelihood of needing more invasive interventions in the future, and contribute to overall health maintenance throughout a patient’s life.[1][2]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam: What to Expect & How Often You Need One.”  
[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lenses: Types & Care” and related vision-care guidance.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic as the major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center and explicitly link it to guidance on annual eye exams in the context of contact lens care. Both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Both responses state that annual eye exams help catch vision problems early, preserve long-term eye health, and are especially important for contact lens users. However, Agent B provides greater depth by specifying particular diseases (glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy) and explicitly tying early detection to reduced risk of permanent vision loss. B also expands on systemic health detection (e.g., diabetes, hypertension), reinforcing long-term benefits in a more comprehensive way.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Agent A uses slightly stronger visual formatting (bold text and clear references), but Agent B delivers richer, more detailed health context and clearer long-term framing (visual preservation, avoidance of invasive interventions, lifelong health maintenance). The added specificity improves informational value without excessive verbosity.

Since both are accurate and complete, but Agent B provides more comprehensive and context-rich benefits while maintaining clarity and structure, B offers the better overall user experience.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.answer_a:
The institution is **Cleveland Clinic**, the Ohio-based nonprofit academic medical center that says annual eye exams can catch vision changes early and support long-term eye health, including for contact lens users.[1][2]

Cleveland Clinic organizes its care information through a broad **Departments, Institutes & Centers** structure on its website, where patients can browse clinical areas alphabetically or by specialty. This directory presents major medical and surgical departments, multidisciplinary institutes, and center-based programs, each linking to condition pages, treatments, locations, and appointment options. Its **Services** and specialty pages further group care by disease area and patient need, helping users navigate from general service lines to specific programs and providers.[3][4]

Its eye care is delivered through the **Cole Eye Institute**. Cleveland Clinic states that its eye team includes **ophthalmologists** and **optometrists** working together. Ophthalmologists are medical doctors and surgeons who diagnose eye disease, prescribe medication, and perform procedures and surgery; optometrists provide routine eye care, vision testing, refractions, prescriptions for glasses and contact lenses, and evaluation/management of many common eye problems.[5][6] Services described by Cleveland Clinic include comprehensive eye exams, contact lens exams, pediatric and adult eye care, cataract care, glaucoma, cornea and external disease, retina care, refractive surgery such as LASIK evaluation, oculoplastics, neuro-ophthalmology, and treatment of routine vision issues and complex ocular disease.[5][7]

Its **Virtual Second Opinions** program lets patients submit records online for review by a Cleveland Clinic specialist. After selecting a specialty, patients create an account, complete a questionnaire, upload medical records/imaging, and pay the fee; a written expert opinion is then returned through the platform.[8] Cleveland Clinic lists **$565 for expert review** and **$780 with a video visit**.[8] The program generally **is not covered by insurance** and is self-pay.[8] It is available to **patients in the U.S. and internationally**, with some limitations based on specialty and location.[8]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam  
[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24445-eye-exam  
[3] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments, Institutes & Centers.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments  
[4] Cleveland Clinic. “Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/services  
[5] Cleveland Clinic. “Cole Eye Institute.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye  
[6] Cleveland Clinic. “Optometrist vs. Ophthalmologist.” https://health.clevelandclinic.org/optometrist-vs-ophthalmologist  
[7] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Care Services / Vision and Eye Conditions” pages, Cole Eye Institute. https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye  
[8] Cleveland Clinic. “Virtual Second Opinions.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/virtual-second-opinions
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic, a major U.S. nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio, advises patients to have annual eye exams to detect vision problems early and preserve long‑term eye health—especially important for current or prospective contact lens wearers.[1][2]

1. **Organization of departments and services**  
Cleveland Clinic presents its clinical offerings through a systemwide “Institutes & Departments” structure (e.g., Cole Eye Institute, Heart, Cancer, Neurological institutes). Each institute page lists its subspecialty departments, conditions treated, tests and procedures, locations, and provider directories, with patient‑facing overviews, appointment links, and educational articles integrated on condition- and service‑specific pages.[3]

2. **Eye care professionals, qualifications, and services**  
Eye care is centered in the Cole Eye Institute, staffed by board‑certified ophthalmologists, optometrists, and specialized surgeons (cornea, retina, glaucoma, oculoplastics, pediatric, refractive, and cataract).[4] Services span routine eye and contact lens exams, refractions, and contact lens fittings to advanced care for cataracts, glaucoma, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, corneal disease, uveitis, pediatric eye disorders, and refractive surgery (LASIK/PRK).[2][4] Providers manage medical and surgical treatment, prescribe corrective lenses, monitor chronic eye disease, and coordinate multidisciplinary care when systemic disease is involved.[2][4]

3. **Virtual Second Opinion program**  
Cleveland Clinic’s Virtual Second Opinions program (powered by The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic) allows patients to upload records, then be matched with a Cleveland Clinic expert for an in‑depth chart review and written opinion; many packages include a video consultation.[5] As of 2026, self‑pay pricing is commonly listed around $1,850 for adults, with lower pediatric and add‑on tiers; fees cover records collection, case navigation, and the specialist’s review.[5] Insurance typically does not cover the service; patients pay out‑of‑pocket, though they may use HSA/FSA funds.[5] The program is available to most U.S. residents (state‑dependent) and many international patients, with dedicated intake workflows and translated support for select regions.[5]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic – “Eye Exam: What to Expect & How Often You Need One.”  
[2] Cleveland Clinic – “Contact Lenses: Types & Care.”  
[3] Cleveland Clinic – “Institutes & Departments” overview pages.  
[4] Cleveland Clinic – “Cole Eye Institute” and related clinical program pages.  
[5] Cleveland Clinic / The Clinic – “Virtual Second Opinions” program description and FAQ.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Cleveland Clinic as the institution and appropriately link annual eye exams, the Cole Eye Institute, and the Virtual Second Opinions (VSO) program to Cleveland Clinic. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both fail key checklist items related to the Virtual Second Opinions program. Agent A provides clearly incorrect pricing ($565/$780), which significantly contradicts the required tiers ($1,690; $1,990; $4,500 international). Agent B also provides incorrect and vague pricing (~$1,850) and omits the required detailed tier breakdown, international $4,500 fee, and explicit state exclusions. Neither agent clearly specifies the structured process in the required detail (registration, records collection, expert report, optional visit) nor do they adequately cover state-by-state limitations or country exclusions. Thus, both have significant factual gaps and inaccuracies in a critical section.

Third, Presentation & UX: Both are well-structured, readable, and use headings effectively. Agent A provides more specific organizational detail and slightly richer service descriptions, while Agent B mentions "The Clinic" branding but lacks pricing precision. However, because both contain substantial factual errors in pricing and program specifics, neither can be considered high-quality.

Conclusion: Since both agents correctly identify the institution but both substantially fail the required pricing and availability details (major WIDTH failures), this is a LOW-QUALITY TIE.

rounds.answer_a:
The organization is **Cleveland Clinic**, a major U.S.-based **nonprofit academic medical center**. In its patient guidance on contact lens exams and routine vision care, Cleveland Clinic recommends getting an **annual eye exam**, even if you do not notice obvious changes in your sight.[1][2]

Its reasoning is preventive: yearly exams can help detect **vision changes early**, confirm whether your prescription is still accurate, and identify whether contact lenses continue to fit properly and safely.[1] Cleveland Clinic also says routine eye exams are important because they can uncover **eye diseases and other health problems before symptoms become more noticeable or serious**.[2]

For people who wear or are considering contact lenses, this advice is especially relevant because some issues—such as improper lens fit, irritation, or subtle shifts in vision—may develop gradually and go unnoticed without regular evaluation.[1] By encouraging annual exams, Cleveland Clinic highlights the long-term benefit of helping patients **keep their eyes healthy for years to come**, while also improving the chance of early treatment if a problem appears.[2]

So, the institution referred to is **Cleveland Clinic**, and its recommendation is to schedule a **yearly eye exam** as a proactive way to catch problems early, maintain clear vision, and support long-term eye health.[1][2]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam

[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Eye Exam.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/24445-eye-exam
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic, a major U.S.-based nonprofit academic medical center headquartered in Ohio, is the organization that explicitly recommends having an eye exam every year to support long-term eye health and early detection of vision problems.[1][2] In its patient education on eye exams and vision care, Cleveland Clinic states that getting your eyes checked annually “can help catch vision problems early and keep your eyes healthy for years to come.”[1]

The Clinic explains that yearly comprehensive eye exams allow eye-care providers to identify subtle changes in vision, update prescriptions, and ensure that any vision correction method—such as contact lenses—remains safe and effective over time.[1][2] They emphasize that many serious eye conditions, including glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, cataracts, and macular degeneration, often develop with few or no early symptoms, so regular exams are critical for detecting these diseases before they cause irreversible damage.[1]

Cleveland Clinic also notes that annual exams can reveal signs of systemic health problems like diabetes and high blood pressure by examining blood vessels and other structures in the eye, helping protect both ocular and overall health.[1][2] By promoting yearly eye examinations as a standard part of preventive care, the institution underscores that consistent monitoring is key to preserving clear vision, reducing the risk of long-term complications, and maintaining healthy eyes for years to come.[1]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic – “Eye Exam: What to Expect & How Often You Need One.”  
[2] Cleveland Clinic – “Contact Lenses: Types & Care” and related vision-care guidance.

Agent B also directly mirrors the quoted phrasing from the guidance, enhancing alignment with the query. Both are well-formatted and readable, but Agent B offers greater informational depth and practical context, making it marginally more helpful overall.

Therefore, Agent B wins on overall user utility and comprehensiveness, though both responses are high quality.

rounds.answer_a:
The institution is **Cleveland Clinic** in Ohio. For patients seeking eye exams and vision correction, Cleveland Clinic offers care through **optometrists and ophthalmologists**. Its Cole Eye Institute provides routine and specialty eye care, including **comprehensive eye exams, contact lens exams, glasses and contact lens prescriptions, LASIK evaluations, cataract care, and treatment for a wide range of eye diseases and conditions**.[1][2] Its optometrists handle vision testing, refractive care, and many general eye-health needs, while ophthalmologists provide medical and surgical eye care.[1][2]

Cleveland Clinic’s **MyConsult Online Medical Second Opinion** is a **virtual second opinion** service that lets patients submit records remotely for review by a Cleveland Clinic specialist.[3] The process generally involves: creating an account, choosing a specialty, completing an online medical questionnaire, uploading relevant records/images/test results, paying the fee, and then receiving a written specialist opinion through the secure portal.[3] Cleveland Clinic states that the program is available to **patients in the U.S. and internationally**.[3] Pricing varies by service level: a **physician second opinion** is listed at **$565**, while a **nurse review** costs **$185**.[3] Cleveland Clinic notes that **MyConsult is typically not covered by insurance**, so patients usually pay out of pocket.[3]

For a broad guide to additional specialty care—useful if a patient wants to explore related services for eye disease, corneal issues, infections, or other complications—Cleveland Clinic provides a comprehensive **Departments, Institutes & Services** directory on its website. That directory organizes the hospital’s clinical offerings across specialties and links to service-line pages, including eye care and many other departments and institutes.[4]

## References
[1] Cleveland Clinic. “Contact Lens Exam: What to Expect, Types, Cost & More.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diagnostics/contact-lens-exam

[2] Cleveland Clinic. “Cole Eye Institute.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye

[3] Cleveland Clinic. “MyConsult Online Medical Second Opinion.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/online-services/myconsult

[4] Cleveland Clinic. “Departments, Institutes & Services.” https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments
rounds.answer_b:
Cleveland Clinic is the major nonprofit academic medical center in Ohio that recommends annual eye exams to catch vision problems early and keep eyes healthy for years to come.[1] Its Cole Eye Institute provides care from ophthalmologists (medical and surgical eye doctors), optometrists (primary vision care, refractions, contact lens fitting), and opticians (eyeglass fabrication and fitting).[2] Services include comprehensive eye exams, contact lens evaluations, cataract and refractive surgery consultations, management of glaucoma, retina and cornea diseases, pediatric eye care, and low‑vision rehabilitation.[2]

The Cleveland Clinic Virtual Second Opinions program (administered with The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic) allows patients to get an expert review of a diagnosis or treatment plan without traveling.[3] Key steps: (1) online enrollment and medical questionnaire; (2) medical record upload/collection coordinated by the program; (3) case matching with a Cleveland Clinic specialist; (4) thorough chart review; (5) a live video visit with the specialist; and (6) delivery of a written report that can be shared with local providers.[3] Pricing is typically a flat self‑pay fee (commonly in the ~$1,850 range for most conditions, with some pediatric or complex cases higher), which generally isn’t covered by insurance, FSA/HSA use may be possible, and the service is available to most U.S. states and many international locations, with some regulatory exclusions.[3][4]

To explore broader specialty care (for example, neurology for optic nerve issues, rheumatology for autoimmune eye disease, or infectious disease for severe contact‑lens–related infections), patients can use Cleveland Clinic’s centralized “Institutes & Departments” or “Clinical Institutes” directory, which lists all hospitals, institutes, departments, and clinical services system‑wide.[5]

## References  
[1] Cleveland Clinic – “Eye Exam: What to Expect & How Often You Need One.”  
[2] Cleveland Clinic – Cole Eye Institute patient information pages.  
[3] The Clinic by Cleveland Clinic – “Virtual Second Opinions” program description.  
[4] Cleveland Clinic / The Clinic – Pricing and FAQ for virtual second opinions.  
[5] Cleveland Clinic – “Institutes & Departments” (systemwide clinical services overview).
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified Cleveland Clinic (passes DEEP logic). However, Agent A failed key WIDTH checklist requirements by describing the outdated MyConsult program with incorrect pricing ($565/$185) and omitting the required Virtual Second Opinions details (The Clinic partnership, $1,690/$1,990 U.S. tiers, $4,500 international tier, Medicare non-reimbursement, state/country exclusions, nurse intake workflow). These are major factual errors. Agent B correctly referenced the Cleveland Clinic Virtual Second Opinions program (The Clinic), outlined the core workflow steps (enrollment, record collection, specialist matching, written report, live visit), and addressed insurance and geographic availability, though pricing lacked exact tier specificity. While not perfect, Agent B is substantially closer to the ground truth and provides a clearer, more structured explanation. Therefore, B wins on accuracy and overall user experience.
